topic: Two friends both apply for the same job
raw_output:
[story 1]

Liam stared at the acceptance email. His thumb hovered over the 'open' button, then retreated. Across the desk, Maya was already laughing, a bright, unrestrained sound that made Liam’s chest feel tight. He reread the email, the words swimming before his eyes. He had poured weeks into tailoring his application, highlighting every relevant skill, yet Maya’s submission seemed to have landed with an almost effortless grace. A slow, heavy sigh escaped him, the air in the small office suddenly thick. He leaned back in his chair, the springs groaning in protest, and began methodically sorting through his notes, the pen scratching a frantic rhythm against the paper, unable to focus on anything else.

[story 2]

"You got it, Liam!" Maya beamed, slapping his shoulder with genuine enthusiasm. Liam managed a tight smile, the muscles in his jaw feeling unexpectedly strained. He looked down at the printed confirmation, the ink seeming to mock his efforts. He picked up his coffee mug, the ceramic warm against his palms, and took a deliberate, long sip, letting the heat burn a path down his throat. He found himself tapping his fingers against the edge of the table, a rapid, uneven pattern. *How could she have done that?* The thought echoed in the quiet hum of the building. He stood up abruptly, needing space, and walked toward the window, watching the city lights blur into streaks of indifferent color.

[story 3]

I reread the notification three times. The screen glowed, reflecting faintly in my glasses. My hands, resting on the keyboard, started to tremble almost imperceptibly. Maya had been so confident during our mock interview preparation; she’d practically radiated assurance. Now, seeing her name listed first, a strange, hollow sensation settled in my stomach. I pushed my chair back, the scrape of metal on linoleum sounding unnaturally loud. I walked to the water cooler, gripping the cold metal pitcher until my knuckles were white. I didn't look at Maya, who was already chatting animatedly with a colleague nearby. I just stood there, feeling a sudden, profound disconnect, as if the ground beneath my feet had momentarily shifted.
